skill.name: reviewdog
skill.description: Configure reviewdog for automated security and quality review in CI/CD. The skill provides GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit patterns for turning scanner output into pull request comments.
skill.summary: Set up reviewdog to publish security scanner and linter findings in code review workflows.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and token patterns. Most are expected for a reviewdog CI integration skill, but the GitLab template includes a confirmed curl-to-shell installer pattern that should be remediated before publication. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
high_findings.description: The GitLab CI template downloads the reviewdog install script over the network and executes it directly with sh. This is a real supply chain risk because CI would execute remote script contents without pinning, checksum verification, or review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is confirmed in a CI template and directly combines network download with shell execution. The use is probably intended for installation, but the execution pattern is unsafe for reusable marketplace content.
high_findings.remediation: Replace curl-to-shell with a pinned release download, package manager install, or verified checksum workflow.

medium_findings.title: Repository Token Use Requires Least Privilege Controls
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions examples pass repository tokens to reviewdog so it can post pull request comments and checks. This is legitimate for reviewdog, but users must keep permissions minimal and avoid exposing tokens to untrusted pull request code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token references are clearly present and required for reviewdog reporters. Risk depends on repository settings, pull request trust boundaries, and scanner behavior rather than malicious code in the skill.
medium_findings.remediation: Document least privilege permissions and avoid running token-bearing jobs on untrusted pull request code.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Scanner Pipelines Run In User Repositories
medium_findings.description: The skill provides shell pipelines that run scanners and pass results to reviewdog. This matches the skill purpose, but users should review scanner arguments, pinned tool versions, and file write locations before adopting the templates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution patterns are real but primarily demonstrate normal reviewdog usage. They become risky if copied into CI without pinning tools or reviewing behavior on untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.remediation: Pin scanner versions, review shell commands before use, and run templates first in a test branch.

low_findings.title: Security Keyword Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, eval, private key, and command injection terms in reference material. These references describe vulnerability classes and scanner coverage, not instructions to perform unsafe actions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is CWE and supported-tool documentation. The flagged terms are examples of issues that scanners detect.
low_findings.remediation: No security fix is required for these documentation references.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point To Public Documentation And Tool Sources
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are mainly public project, documentation, and pre-commit repository URLs. They do not show data exfiltration endpoints or suspicious callback behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible configuration and documentation references. No evidence found that they receive secrets or repository data.
low_findings.remediation: Keep third-party references current and prefer pinned versions where templates install tools.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Is Normal Scanner Input And Artifact Handling
low_findings.description: Filesystem detections mostly reflect scanner access to repository files, Docker volume mounts, and temporary report files. This behavior is expected for SAST, secret scanning, and linter integrations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are used to scan project contents or store local scan reports. No evidence found of unauthorized file collection or exfiltration.
low_findings.remediation: Users should review mounted paths and report destinations before enabling the templates in CI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The GitLab CI template downloads the reviewdog install script over the network and executes it directly with sh. This is a real supply chain risk because CI would execute remote script contents without pinning, checksum verification, or review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is confirmed in a CI template and directly combines network download with shell execution. The use is probably intended for installation, but the execution pattern is unsafe for reusable marketplace content.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Replace curl-to-shell with a pinned release download, package manager install, or verified checksum workflow.

content.value_statement: Security findings often reach developers too late in the review cycle. This skill helps configure reviewdog so scanner and linter results appear directly in pull requests.
actual_capabilities: Explains reviewdog workflows for turning scanner output into review comments.
Provides GitHub Actions examples for Bandit, Semgrep, Gitleaks, Hadolint, Checkov, and ShellCheck.
Provides GitLab CI examples for merge request security discussions.
Includes pre-commit hook examples for local reviewdog feedback.
Maps common scanner findings to CWE and security categories.
Documents reporter formats and when each reviewdog reporter is useful.
limitations: Does not run scanners or validate results by itself.
Requires reviewdog and each selected scanner to be installed or available in CI.
Templates need repository-specific permission and branch protection review.
Scanner accuracy depends on selected rules, language support, and exclusions.

use_cases.title: Add Pull Request Security Comments
use_cases.description: Configure CI so new SAST and secret findings appear as inline review comments.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Linting Feedback
use_cases.description: Use one reviewdog flow to publish output from several linters and scanners.

use_cases.title: Shift Findings Into Local Development
use_cases.description: Add pre-commit reviewdog hooks so developers see scanner feedback before opening a pull request.

prompt_templates.title: Choose A Basic Reviewdog Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a simple reviewdog setup for a GitHub repository that uses Python and pull requests.

prompt_templates.title: Create A GitHub Actions Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Add CI review comments for common scanners.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a GitHub Actions reviewdog workflow for Semgrep, Bandit, Gitleaks, and ShellCheck with least privilege permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Findings For Developer Signal
prompt_templates.scenario: Reduce noisy comments while keeping security coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my reviewdog plan and suggest filter modes, severity thresholds, and scanner exclusions for fewer false positives.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Multi-Repo Rollout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced platform rollout across many services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a phased reviewdog rollout for GitHub and GitLab repositories, including token permissions, branch protections, scanner pinning, and failure policies.

output_examples.input: A team wants Bandit and Semgrep findings in GitHub pull requests.
output: A workflow plan that installs reviewdog and the scanners.
Recommended permissions for pull request comments and checks.
A severity policy that blocks critical findings and comments on warnings.

output_examples.input: A platform team has too many scanner comments on large pull requests.
output: A tuning plan that uses added-line or diff-context filtering.
Suggested scanner exclusions for generated files and test fixtures.
Guidance for moving full scans to scheduled jobs.

output_examples.input: A developer wants local feedback before pushing code.
output: A pre-commit strategy using reviewdog local reporter mode.
Advice for keeping hooks fast enough for daily development.

best_practices: Pin scanner, action, and container versions before using templates in production.
Use least privilege tokens and avoid token-bearing jobs for untrusted pull request code.
Start with added-line filtering, then expand coverage after false positives are understood.
anti_patterns: Do not copy CI templates without reviewing token permissions and runner trust boundaries.
Do not run every expensive scanner on every pull request without caching or scope controls.
Do not treat reviewdog comments as proof that a repository is vulnerability free.

faq.question: What does this skill help configure?
faq.answer: It helps configure reviewdog to publish scanner and linter findings in pull requests, checks, merge requests, and local output.

faq.question: Does it replace security scanners?
faq.answer: No. Reviewdog receives output from tools such as Semgrep, Bandit, Gitleaks, Hadolint, Checkov, and ShellCheck.

faq.question: Can it block unsafe pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show failure policies for high severity findings, but teams must tune thresholds for their workflow.

faq.question: Does it work with GitLab?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes GitLab CI patterns for merge request discussions and reviewdog reporter settings.

faq.question: What permissions are usually needed?
faq.answer: GitHub examples need read access to contents and write access for pull requests or checks, depending on reporter mode.

faq.question: What should be reviewed before adoption?
faq.answer: Review token exposure, runner trust, pinned tool versions, scanner scope, failure behavior, and any remote installation steps.
